quotes.text: Hatred is never appeased by hatred in this world. By non-hatred alone is hatred appeased. This is an eternal law.
All conditioned things are impermanent. When one sees this with wisdom, one turns away from suffering.
You yourself must strive. The Buddhas only point the way.
Just as a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, so the wise are not moved by praise or blame.
Health is the greatest gift, contentment the greatest wealth, faithfulness the best relationship.
Better than a thousand hollow words is one word that brings peace.
Conquer anger with non-anger. Conquer badness with goodness. Conquer meanness with generosity. Conquer dishonesty with truth.
The mind is the forerunner of all actions. All deeds are led by mind, created by mind.
As a mother would protect her only child, even at the risk of her own life, cultivate a boundless love toward all beings.
Do not dwell in the past, do not dream of the future, concentrate the mind on the present moment.
Holding on to anger is like grasping a hot coal with the intent of throwing it at someone else; you are the one who gets burned.
No one saves us but ourselves. No one can and no one may. We ourselves must walk the path.
In the sky, there is no distinction of east and west; people create distinctions out of their own minds and then believe them to be true.
Three things cannot be long hidden: the sun, the moon, and the truth.
Peace comes from within. Do not seek it without.
The root of suffering is attachment.
You will not be punished for your anger; you will be punished by your anger.
Thousands of candles can be lighted from a single candle, and the life of the candle will not be shortened. Happiness never decreases by being shared.
If you light a lamp for somebody, it will also brighten your path.
The tongue like a sharp knife kills without drawing blood.
A jug fills drop by drop.
Even as a solid rock is unshaken by the wind, so are the wise unshaken by praise and blame.
Whatever is not yours, let go of it. Your letting go will be for your long-term happiness.
Radiate boundless love towards the entire world.
To keep the body in good health is a duty, otherwise we shall not be able to keep our mind strong and clear.
There is no path to happiness. Happiness is the path.
The way is not in the sky. The way is in the heart.
Every morning we are born again. What we do today is what matters most.
If we could see the miracle of a single flower clearly, our whole life would change.
Nothing is permanent. Everything is subject to change. Being is always becoming.
A disciplined mind brings happiness.
Arise! Sit up! Train yourself strenuously for peace.
The wise are controlled in body, controlled in speech, and controlled in mind. They are truly well controlled.
Let none find fault with others; let none see the omissions and commissions of others. But let one see one's own acts, done and undone.
As rain breaks through an ill-thatched house, passion will break through an unreflecting mind.
As rain does not break through a well-thatched house, passion will not break through a well-reflecting mind.
The one in whom no longer exist the craving and thirst that perpetuate becoming; how could you track that Awakened one, trackless, and of limitless range?
Better it is to live one day seeing the rise and fall of things than to live a hundred years without ever seeing the rise and fall of things.
If you knew what I know about the power of giving, you would not let a single meal pass without sharing it in some way.
Give, even if you only have a little.
Should a person do good, let him do it again and again. Let him find pleasure therein, for blissful is the accumulation of good.
Should a person commit evil, let him not do it again and again. Let him not find pleasure therein, for painful is the accumulation of evil.
Like a beautiful flower full of color but without fragrance, fine words are fruitless in a person who does not act in accordance with them.
Like a beautiful flower, full of color but also fragrant, are the fine and fruitful words of one who acts accordingly.
The fragrance of flowers blows with the wind. The fragrance of virtue blows against the wind. The virtuous man pervades every direction.
As a bee gathering nectar does not harm or disturb the color and fragrance of the flower, so do the wise move through the world.
Not by passing arbitrary judgments does a man become just; a wise man is he who investigates both right and wrong.
He who does not rouse himself when it is time to rise, who, though young and strong, is full of sloth, whose will and thought are weak — that lazy and idle man will never find the way to wisdom.
Watchfulness is the path of immortality; unwatchfulness is the path of death. The watchful do not die; the unwatchful are as if dead already.
Those who are ever vigilant, who train day and night, and are ever intent upon Nirvana, their passions pass away.
There is no fire like lust, no grip like hate, no net like delusion, no river like craving.
Easily seen is the fault of others, but one's own fault is difficult to see. Like chaff one winnows another's faults, but hides one's own.
The faults of others are easier to see than one's own; the faults of others are easily seen, for they are sifted out like chaff, but one's own faults are hard to see.
If a man speaks or acts with an evil thought, pain follows him, as the wheel follows the foot of the ox that draws the carriage.
If a man speaks or acts with a pure thought, happiness follows him, like a shadow that never leaves him.
He abused me, he struck me, he overpowered me, he robbed me. Those who harbor such thoughts do not still their hatred.
He abused me, he struck me, he overpowered me, he robbed me. Those who do not harbor such thoughts still their hatred.
For hatred does not cease by hatred at any time; hatred ceases by love. This is an unalterable law.
Many do not know that we are here in this world to live in harmony. Those who know it and those who follow it, their quarrels are soon ended.
As a solid rock is not shaken by the wind, wise people falter not amidst blame and praise.
Like a beautiful flower, bright and perfumed, but fruitless, are the beautiful but empty words of one who does not act accordingly.
The wise man, who by watchfulness conquers thoughtlessness, is as one free from sorrows, and ascends to the palace of wisdom.
The wise man, who by watchfulness conquers thoughtlessness, is as one free from sorrows, and ascends to the palace of wisdom.
When the wise man drives out selfishness by wisdom, as one drives out the most impure thing, then, being free from sorrow, he ascends to the palace of wisdom.
The fool who knows his foolishness is wise at least so far. But a fool who thinks himself wise, he is called a fool indeed.
Though month after month for a hundred years one should offer sacrifices by the thousands, yet if only for a moment one should worship those of perfect mind, that worship is indeed better.
If one man conquers in battle a thousand times a thousand men, and another conquers himself, he is the greatest of conquerors.
One's own self conquered is better than all other people; not even a god, a Gandharva, or Mara with Brahma, can change into defeat the victory of a man who has vanquished himself.
Better than a thousand verses where no profit is derived from hearing them, is one verse on hearing which a man is pacified.
Better than a hundred years in the life of a man who is idle and inactive, is a day in the life of a man who is strenuous and active.
If by sacrificing a limited pleasure one sees a great pleasure, let the wise man sacrifice the limited pleasure, looking to the great pleasure.
Let none find fault with others; let none see the commissions and omissions of others. But let one see one's own acts, done and undone.
Like a beautiful flower, bright and perfumed, but fruitless, are the beautiful but empty words of one who does not act accordingly.
As from a heap of flowers many a garland can be made, even so many good deeds should be done by one born a mortal.
Not the perversities of others, not their sins of commission or omission, but one's own misdeeds and negligences should one regard.
The scent of flowers does not travel against the wind, but the odor of good people travels even against the wind; a good man pervades every place.
Of all the fragrances — sandalwood, tagara, blue lotus, or jasmine — the fragrance of virtue is by far the sweetest.
Faint is the fragrance of tagara and sandalwood, but the fragrance of the virtuous is the sweetest; it spreads even to the abodes of the gods.
The good shine from afar, like the snowy mountains; the bad are not seen, like arrows shot at night.
He who sits alone, rests alone, walks alone, resolute, who in himself has become tranquil, will find delight in the forest.
Delight in meditation, delight in solitude, delight in the Dhamma, the Awakened One's disciples delight.
The one who has conquered himself is a far greater hero than he who has defeated a thousand times a thousand men.
Train yourself in doing good which remains with you always. There is nothing so good as the practice of Dhamma.
The wise man who is restrained in body, restrained in speech, restrained in mind, who is at peace, who has rejected the baits of the world, is called a sage.
He who has no attachment whatsoever for the mind and body, who does not grieve for what he has not — he is truly called a monk.
He who lives in the Dhamma, delights in the Dhamma, meditates on the Dhamma, and bears the Dhamma well in mind — he does not fall away from the sublime Dhamma.
The scent of flowers blows with the wind. The scent of virtue blows against the wind. The virtuous man pervades every direction.
As the jasmine creeper sheds its withered flowers, even so should one shed passion and hatred.
Calm in mind, calm in speech, calm in deed, who rightly knowing is wholly freed, perfectly peaceful and equipoised.
The man who is without blind faith, who knows the uncreated, who has severed all ties, removed all temptations, renounced all desires — he is the greatest of men.
In a hamlet or in a forest, on land or on sea, wherever venerable persons dwell, that place is delightful.
Forest leaves are pleasant; so is the forest. But the forest holds no delight for those who have not tasted the bliss of Dhamma.
Better than a thousand hollow verses is one verse that brings peace.
Though one should live a hundred years immoral and uncontrolled, yet better, indeed, is a life of one day, moral and meditative.
Though one should live a hundred years, not seeing the rise and fall of things, yet better, indeed, is a life of one day, seeing the rise and fall of things.
If by renouncing a lesser happiness one may realize a greater happiness, let the wise man renounce the lesser, having regard for the greater.
Let one not neglect one's own welfare for the sake of another, however great. Clearly understanding one's own welfare, let one be intent upon the good.
Let none find fault with others; let none see the omissions and commissions of others. But let one see one's own acts, done and undone.
All tremble at violence; all fear death. Putting oneself in the place of another, one should not kill nor cause another to kill.
